title: Washington DC · Forty Years of Crime
subtitle: FBI UCR 1985–2007 + MPD incidents 2008–2026 · 46 neighborhood clusters · real locations

hook.line: Washington DC's reported crime, from the 1993 peak to 2007
hook.sub: FBI UCR · and the fall continued — mapped to this week

punchline.sub: −48% under the same FBI measure · the modern record falls another 29%

copy.chapter2Kicker: CHAPTER 2 · THE LAST 5 YEARS · MPD OPEN DATA
copy.chapter2Caption: Monthly reports, 2021–2026. Every dot is a real reported incident location (block-level, sampled).
copy.transitionDesc: The chart counted per year. From here the map counts per month across 46 neighborhood clusters — every dot a real reported location, anonymized to the block.
copy.methodRecentSub: the last five years · month by month · real locations
copy.methodDotsSub: Every dot is a real reported incident location (block-level). 99.2% map to a neighborhood cluster — the rest counted, disclosed, never invented.
copy.methodFootnote: FBI UCR (through 2007) and MPD incident data (2008+) are different systems — shown as two chapters, not one line.
copy.seamExplain: The ruler changes here — not the city. Through 2007 the gray bars are DC's figures reported to the FBI's UCR program — yearly totals of classic “index” crimes. From 2008 the numbers come from MPD's own incident feed: a different source and taxonomy, logged case by case, but still major Part-I-style offenses — so the jump is the measuring system changing, not a re-classification of crime. Compare the shape within one color, never across the dashed line.
copy.quizQuestion: Which DC neighborhood is the safest?
copy.sourceLine: Data: Metropolitan Police Department via Open Data DC (CC BY 4.0) · block-level locations
copy.coverageText: 99.2% of records mapped to a neighborhood cluster
copy.creditsSources: Sources: Metropolitan Police Department crime incidents (2008–present, CC BY 4.0) via Open Data DC & FBI UCR (1985–2007) via the FBI Crime Data Explorer. Neighborhood clusters: DC Office of Planning. DC publishes only major (Part I-style) offenses — drug/vice categories are not published, not zero. Basemap: © OpenStreetMap contributors (ODbL).

historyNotes.text: 1993: the peak — 68,146 crimes, 16,888 of them violent.
The fall runs through the 2000s, year after year.
2007: 35,706 — down 48% from the peak; the city's own record takes over.
The modern record keeps falling through the 2010s.

annotations.text: Every dot is a real reported incident location — anonymized to the block by MPD. DC publishes only major (Part I-style) offenses.
July 2023 — the busiest month of the last five years: 3,398 reports.
Union Station / Stanton Park — DC's busiest area for reported crime.
Capitol View / Marshall Heights: down 33% since 2022 — the city's biggest drop.
Citywide, mapped reports fell 28.5% from 2008 to 2025 (33,780 → 24,153).
